regra: Não copiar. Converter em abertura, pergunta, objeção, follow-up ou fechamento premium.
buscar: tactical empathy customer experience premium service
entrega: ideia central + aplicação WhatsApp + script antes/depois + métrica + risco

coleta.cmd: python3 /root/.openclaw/workspace/skills/youtube-learning-ivs/scripts/youtube_learning.py search --topic Chris Voss Shep Hyken tactical empathy customer experience premium service --limit 3
coleta.stdout:
, Shep Hyken discusses going the extra mile.\n\nGoing the extra mile is an old expression. It describes people who provide better customer service, do a little more than expected and try a little harder. This is a great concept for customer service and is further enhanced by Roger Staubach, the Hall-of-Fame football player who played quarterback for the Dallas Cowboys, who said:\n\nThere are no traffic jams along the extra mile.\n\nThis is a great metaphor that can be used in a variety of ways and is especially applicable to business, and specifically the concept of the customer experience (CX).\n\nGoing the extra mile is about the “baker’s dozen,” which is getting more than you thought you were paying for. It’s the extra time a salesperson spends helping  you make the right selection. It’s the customer support rep that takes an extra few minutes to ensure you have all of your questions answered and won’t need to call back. You get the idea.\n\nI was talking to a client, Chris Cielewich at FLAVORx, about this very concept. FLAVORx is a machine that takes terrible tasting medications and flavors them. Parents love this, because kids are more willing to take their medicine. Sounds good so far. There is a catch, and here is where the concept of the extra mile comes into play.\n\nIf the customer asks for the FLAVORx, it takes the pharmacist an extra 45 seconds to fill the prescription. Some pharmacies are so focused on productivity that they don’t want to take the extra 45 seconds, even if it creates a better customer experience.\n\nChris Cielewich conducted an experiment. He had three prescriptions to fill for his children and decided to go to three different pharmacies to see which pharmacist would recommend FLAVORx. With the kids in tow, he ventured out to fill the prescriptions. None of the pharmacists went the extra mile to ask if the kids wanted their meds flavored. When Chris asked them to flavor the medicine all three complied, but only one did so with a smile. The other two begrudgingly commented about how busy they were; obviously too busy to go the extra mile.\n\nSo what can you do to go the extra mile? Simple: Give the customer more than they thought they were going to get. The old saying of going the extra mile is a sound customer service strategy. It’s taking some extra time, makin",

      "description": "What's the biggest obstacle to delivering great customer service? In this video, we reveal the surprising answer: pride. Drawing ...",
      "description_full": "What’s the biggest obstacle to delivering great customer service? In this video, we reveal the surprising answer: pride. Drawing insights from a conversation with Brian Hamilton, chairman of Livswitch and a seasoned entrepreneur, we explore how pride can undermine even the best customer experience efforts. Whether it’s handling complaints or navigating difficult situations, pride can get in the way of finding proactive solutions and truly winning the customer.\n\nHave you ever dealt with a customer who was clearly wrong, yet you felt compelled to prove your point? That’s pride taking control. Instead of escalating conflicts, this video highlights practical strategies to stay ahead of the competition by focusing on what matters most—helping the customer. Learn to listen without interrupting, choose your words carefully, empathize with their concerns, and prioritize problem-solving over being right. \n\nGreat customer service isn't about winning arguments; it’s about creating positive outcomes. By letting go of pride and adopting a proactive approach, you can elevate your customer experience and build lasting relationships. Ask yourself: what’s more important—being helpful or being right? The answer could transform how you handle complaints and challenges in your business.\n\nWatch now to gain actionable insights and take your customer service to the next level. Don’t forget to reflect on your own experiences, share your thoughts in the comments, and start implementing these tips today to always stay amazing in the eyes of your customers!\n\n#humbleattitude #empathystatementscallcenter #customerservicetipsforbeginners #activelistening #empathystatementsforcustomerservice\n\n#howtohandlecustomercomplaintslikeapro #wincustomers #livechatetiquette #livechatcustomerservice #empathystatementsforcustomerservice\n\nCHAPTERS:\n00:00 - Enemy of Customer Service, Pride\n00:51 - Customer Expectations, Not Always Right\n03:18 - Thank You for Watching, Subscribe",
